advantages: Extremely simple to implement and explain — one weighted sum, one bias, one step — ideal for teaching linear classification and neural network building blocks.
Guaranteed finite-step convergence on linearly separable data (Perceptron Convergence Theorem), with no need for convex QP solvers.
Online, mistake-driven updates suit streaming data and low memory footprints compared to storing full training sets.
Decision boundary wᵀx + b = 0 is interpretable: weights show feature direction and relative importance for the binary split.
Same linear score z = wᵀx + b underlies logistic regression and linear SVM — mastering the perceptron transfers to those models.
disadvantages: Cannot learn non-linearly separable patterns (XOR, overlapping classes) — fails without hidden layers or feature maps.
No margin maximization — many separating hyperplanes exist; the perceptron may pick a boundary that generalizes poorly near class overlap.
Step activation is non-differentiable at z = 0 — blocks gradient-based training of deeper stacks using this unit alone (modern nets use smooth activations).
On non-separable data, weights oscillate indefinitely unless variants (pocket, averaged perceptron) or different losses are used.
Outputs hard 0/1 labels, not calibrated probabilities — unsuitable when risk decisions need P(y=1|x) without a separate model.
